title: Materials and Manufacturing Processes for Visual Testing
narrationScript: A Level Two visual inspector is expected to know more than how to look. You are expected to know what you are looking at, how it was made, and what that history means for the discontinuities you should expect. That is what this lesson is about. We start where every inspection should start, with the drawing. The fabrication drawing tells you the material specification, the joint locations, the weld sizes, and the acceptance criteria that apply. Weld symbols carry a surprising amount of information on one small reference line. The arrow points to the joint. Information below the reference line applies to the arrow side. Information above the line applies to the other side. A flag at the bend means a field weld. A circle at the bend means weld all around. The tail carries the process or specification reference. Before you inspect anything, confirm the drawing revision matches your traveler or work order, find every joint you are responsible for, and plan your access and your lighting. Remember that direct viewing means getting your eye within twenty-four inches of the surface and at an angle of at least thirty degrees to it. If you cannot reach a joint that way, that is the moment to arrange mirrors, borescopes, or staging, not after the crew has closed the area. Next, raw materials. Steel begins as molten metal that solidifies either in an ingot mold or in a continuous caster. As an ingot cools from the outside in, the last metal to freeze sits at the top center, and it shrinks. That shrinkage cavity is called pipe. Ingots also carry segregation, which is uneven chemistry, plus nonmetallic inclusions and gas porosity. The mill crops the worst of the piped top, but whatever survives travels downstream. The ingot or cast strand is worked into three intermediate shapes. Slabs are wide and flat and become plate, sheet, and strip. Blooms are large and roughly square and become structural shapes and rails. Billets are smaller and become bars, rod, wire, seamless tubing, and forging stock. Here is the idea that makes you a better inspector. Working the metal does not erase ingot discontinuities. It reshapes them. Rolling flattens a pipe cavity or a cluster of inclusions into a thin lamination lying parallel to the plate surface, which is why you check plate edges. Surface defects stretch into long tight seams on bars. Inclusions string out into stringers aligned with the working direction. When you know the product form, you know the likely discontinuity and its likely orientation. Each primary process adds its own signature. Forgings can show laps, where a fold of surface metal is pressed in without fusing, and bursts, where the interior ruptures because the metal was worked too cold or too fast. Castings never get worked at all, so they keep their solidification features. Expect gas porosity, shrinkage in thick sections, cold shuts where two streams of metal met without fusing, hot tears at section changes, and sand or slag inclusions. Tubing splits into two families. Seamless tube is pierced from a billet and can carry gouges and seams on both surfaces. Welded tube adds a weld line that needs its own inspection. Extrusions show die lines, which are long fine scratches from the die, plus tearing and blisters. Drawn wire picks up die scratches and slivers. Finally, joining. There are five basic joint configurations. Butt, tee, corner, lap, and edge. Groove preparations such as square, vee, bevel, U shaped, and J shaped grooves exist to let the weld reach the full thickness. Welding fuses the base metal itself. Your visual duties run before, during, and after. Before welding you verify fit up, root opening, alignment, bevel angle, and cleanliness. After welding you evaluate reinforcement, undercut, overlap, porosity, cracks, spatter, and arc strikes. Brazing is different. The filler melts above about four hundred fifty degrees Celsius, the base metal never melts, and capillary action pulls the filler through a close fitting joint. You inspect for a continuous fillet of filler at the joint line, with no voids, no flux residue, and no overheating discoloration. Soldering works on the same principle below four hundred fifty degrees Celsius. A good solder joint is smooth, concave, and shiny. A dull grainy joint suggests a cold joint. Adhesive bonding gives you the least to see. Once the joint closes, the visible evidence is the squeeze out along the edge, so surface preparation must be verified before the parts come together. Learn the history of the part, and the part will tell you where to look.

sections.heading: Start With the Drawing
sections.body:
A visual test plan begins at the documents, not the part. Pull the fabrication drawing, the weld map or isometric, and the applicable procedure, and confirm the **drawing revision** matches your work order before anything else — inspecting to a superseded revision is a classic audit finding.

From the drawing you extract part and joint numbering, material specification, weld sizes and lengths, surface-finish callouts, and the reference to the acceptance criteria. Weld symbols compress most of this onto one reference line:

- The **arrow** points to the joint; a symbol **below** the reference line applies to the **arrow side**, a symbol above the line to the **other side**.
- A filled **flag** at the bend means a field weld; a small **circle** at the bend means weld-all-around.
- The **tail** carries process or specification references.

Then plan the physical examination: which joints permit direct viewing — eye within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface and at not less than 30° to it — where auxiliary lighting will be needed to reach the 500–1000 lux range procedures typically call out, and which joints demand mirrors or a borescope. Booking access, staging, and hold points from the drawing, before fit-up, is what separates a planned examination from a flashlight walk-around.

sections.caption: Anatomy of a weld symbol: arrow-side information sits below the reference line, other-side information above, with the flag, circle, and tail adding field-weld, weld-all-around, and process references.

sections.heading: Raw Materials: Ingots, Blooms, Billets, and Slabs
sections.body:
Steel solidifies either in an **ingot mold** or, far more commonly today, in a **continuous caster** that produces a solid strand cut to length. Either way, solidification leaves a signature:

- **Pipe** — a shrinkage cavity at the top center of an ingot, where the last metal to freeze contracted.
- **Segregation** — uneven distribution of alloying elements and impurities, concentrated where freezing finished.
- **Nonmetallic inclusions** such as slag, oxides, and sulfides, and **gas porosity** trapped in the solid.

The mill crops the piped top of the ingot, but any remnant travels into every product made from it.

The intermediate shapes matter because they tell you what the metal will become:

- **Slab** — wide, flat rectangular section; rolled into plate, sheet, and strip.
- **Bloom** — large, roughly square section; worked into structural shapes and rails.
- **Billet** — smaller square or round section; becomes bars, rod, wire, seamless tubing, and forging stock.

Hot working closes some small voids, but it does not destroy oxide-lined cavities or inclusions — it elongates and flattens them along the working direction. That single fact predicts the orientation of most wrought-product discontinuities you will ever find.

sections.caption: Steel flows from the ingot or continuous-cast strand through slab, bloom, and billet into the product forms VT meets in the shop, carrying reshaped solidification discontinuities with it.

sections.heading: Plate, Forgings, and Castings
sections.body:
**Plate and sheet** are rolled from slabs. Pipe and inclusion clusters roll out into **laminations** — thin internal separations parallel to the surface. They rarely show on the face; you find them on cut edges, at drilled holes, and along sheared edges as a visible parting line. Surface VT targets on rolled product include rolled-in scale, scabs, slivers, and pitting.

**Forgings** are hammered or pressed to shape from billets or blooms. The two signatures are **laps** — folds of surface metal pressed in without fusing, typically at flash lines, fillets, and corners, appearing as smooth, curved, tight linear indications — and **bursts**, internal or surface ruptures from working the metal too cold or too aggressively. Exposed flow lines are normal; cracks following them are not.

**Castings** are never mechanically worked, so they retain solidification features: **gas porosity** (rounded cavities), **shrinkage** in the last-to-freeze thick sections, **cold shuts** — smooth-edged linear indications where two streams of metal met without fusing — **hot tears** at section changes restrained during cooling, and **sand or slag inclusions**. Surface texture varies with the molding process, so procedures often define acceptable roughness using comparator plates before evaluation even starts.

sections.heading: Bars, Tubing, Extrusions, and Wire
sections.body:
**Bars** carry billet defects stretched long and thin. **Seams** are tight, longitudinal, often dead straight, and easy to mistake for a scratch — a scratch usually shows bright, raised, or torn edges, while a seam does not. Rolling laps lie at a shallow angle to the surface.

**Tubing** splits into two families. **Seamless** tube is pierced and rolled from a billet; inspect both surfaces for gouges, seams, and pickup from worn tooling — ID inspection usually means a borescope. **Welded** tube adds a continuous weld line: verify the flash trim on the OD and, where the specification requires it, the ID bead as well.

**Extrusions** are pushed through a die, leaving **die lines** — fine, continuous longitudinal scratches — plus possible surface tearing on thin sections and **blisters** from subsurface gas. The back end of an extrusion can carry piped-in oxide, the so-called back-end defect, and is normally cropped off; verify that it was.

**Wire** is drawn through successive dies. VT targets are die scratches, slivers, and surface cracking; heavy reductions can produce internal chevron-shaped bursts that only show where they break the surface or at a fracture.

sections.title: Discontinuities Are Inherited
sections.body: A lamination in plate is often yesterday's ingot pipe; a stringer was once an inclusion; a seam on a bar began as a crack on an ingot surface. **Process history predicts discontinuity type and orientation**: wrought discontinuities align with the working direction, while casting discontinuities cluster at thick sections and section changes. Ask what the part was made from and how, and you will know where to point the light.

sections.heading: Joining Processes and Joint Configurations
sections.body:
All welded construction reduces to **five basic joints** — butt, tee, corner, lap, and edge. Groove preparations (square, V, bevel, U, and J) exist to give the heat source access to full thickness; fillet welds join tee, lap, and corner configurations without groove preparation. The joint type tells you what to verify at fit-up — root opening, groove angle, alignment (hi-lo), cleanliness — and what to evaluate afterward: reinforcement and convexity, undercut, overlap, surface porosity, cracks, spatter, and arc strikes.

- **Welding** fuses the base metal itself, with or without filler.
- **Brazing** melts only the filler, above about 450 °C (840 °F); the base metal stays solid and **capillary action** draws the filler through a close-fitting joint — typical capillary clearances run only a few hundredths of a millimeter up to roughly 0.15 mm. VT looks for a continuous, concave filler fillet completely around the joint line, with no voids, flux residue, or overheating discoloration.
- **Soldering** uses the same wetting-and-capillary principle below about 450 °C. A sound joint is smooth, concave, and bright; dull and grainy suggests a cold or disturbed joint. On electronics, also look for icicles, bridging, and non-wetting.
- **Adhesive bonding** creates no metallurgical bond, so strength rides on surface preparation and cure. After closure, VT is largely limited to a continuous, uniform **squeeze-out bead** at the edges — which is why prep verification before assembly is itself a visual hold point.

sections.caption: The five basic weld joint configurations and the common groove preparations that define what fit-up VT must verify.

figures.title: Five Basic Weld Joints and Groove Preparations

interactive.title: Which Joining Process Is It?
interactive.intro: Classify each statement or field observation under the joining process it describes.

items.label: The base metal itself melts and fuses, with or without filler
items.why: Fusion of the base metal is the defining feature of welding; brazing, soldering, and bonding never melt the base metal.

items.label: A crater crack is found at the stop point of a pass
items.why: Craters form where a fusion-welding arc stops; crater cracks are a welding discontinuity.

items.label: Filler melts above about 450 °C and is drawn into a close-fitting joint by capillary action
items.why: That temperature threshold with an unmelted base metal and capillary flow is the textbook definition of brazing.

items.label: A silver-alloy filler forms a continuous meniscus around a heat-exchanger fitting
items.why: Silver-base fillers are classic brazing alloys; the continuous visible fillet line is the main brazing VT target.

items.label: Filler melts below about 450 °C, and a dull grainy surface suggests a cold joint
items.why: Soldering works below about 450 °C; cold or disturbed solder joints lose their smooth bright finish.

items.label: Icicles and bridging appear between adjacent terminals on a circuit board
items.why: Icicles and bridging are solder-application defects typical of electronic assemblies.

items.label: Joint strength depends on surface preparation and cure; no metallurgical bond forms
items.why: Adhesive bonds are chemical and mechanical, not metallurgical, so prep and cure control everything.

items.label: After assembly, VT is limited to checking a uniform squeeze-out bead along the edge of a doubler
items.why: Once a bonded joint closes, the squeeze-out at the edges is usually the only direct visual evidence left.

checkQuestions.question: On a weld symbol, a fillet symbol placed below the reference line tells you the weld goes on:
options: The side of the joint opposite the arrow
The arrow side of the joint
checkQuestions.explanation: By convention, information below the reference line applies to the arrow side of the joint; information above the line applies to the other side.

checkQuestions.question: Which intermediate mill form is rolled into plate and sheet?
checkQuestions.explanation: Slabs are the wide, flat rectangular intermediate form; rolling them produces plate, sheet, and strip. Blooms feed structural shapes and billets feed bar, rod, wire, and tube.

checkQuestions.question: A smooth-edged linear indication on a casting surface where two streams of metal met without fusing is a:
checkQuestions.explanation: A cold shut forms when two metal streams meet after losing too much heat to fuse, leaving a smooth, rounded-edge linear discontinuity. Hot tears are ragged and occur at restrained section changes.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly separates brazing from welding?
options: Brazing melts the base metal but not the filler
Brazing only works on aluminum alloys
Brazing requires no heat at all
Brazing melts only the filler, above about 450 °C, and capillary action fills the joint
checkQuestions.explanation: In brazing the base metal never melts; filler melting above about 450 °C (840 °F) is drawn through the close-fitting joint by capillary action. Welding fuses the base metal itself.

checkQuestions.question: Which checks belong to fit-up (before-welding) visual examination?
options: Root opening, groove angle, and hi-lo alignment
checkQuestions.explanation: Root opening, groove angle, alignment, and cleanliness exist only at fit-up — once the weld is in, they can no longer be verified. Undercut, reinforcement, spatter, and arc strikes are after-welding checks.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II Supplement §3.1–3.4
AWS welding symbol and terminology conventions (generic reference, e.g., A2.4 / A3.0)
General manufacturing-process references for wrought and cast product forms
